title: NOC 90011 – Utilities Managers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 90011 (Utilities Managers) are the guardians of Canada’s essential infrastructure, overseeing the distribution of water, gas, and electricity. As Canada transitions toward a net-zero grid, these TEER 0 managers are at the forefront of the green energy revolution. Because of their specialized technical and managerial skill sets, utilities managers are prime candidates for STEM-targeted Express Entry draws and specialized provincial energy streams.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Level0Major Group90 – Management occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group900 – Management occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesMinor Group9001 – Managers in manufacturing and utilitiesNOC Code90011

duties: Plan, organize, and evaluate the operations of utility companies or distribution services.
Develop and implement policies and procedures for the treatment and distribution of utilities.
Manage budgets and oversee the maintenance of transmission lines and distribution equipment.
Hire and supervise technical staff and coordinate with government regulatory agencies.
Respond to emergency situations and ensure the continuity of essential services.
employment_requirements: A bachelor's degree or college diploma in an appropriate discipline (e.g., Electrical Engineering, Water Resource Technology) is required.
Several years of experience as a supervisor in a related utilities operations department are mandatory.
Professional Engineer (P.Eng.) certification is often required for energy-related roles.
job_outlook: The outlook is Good. The clean energy transition ensures that utilities management will remain a high-growth field through 2031 and beyond.
labour_market_demand: The demand is high due to aging infrastructure and the massive shift toward renewable energy sources. Provinces like Alberta (Natural Gas/Wind) and Ontario (Nuclear/Hydro) are aggressively recruiting managers who can lead complex utility projects and distribution networks.
Infrastructure Investment: Direct involvement in multi-billion dollar renewable energy transitions.
Career Prestige: One of the most stable and respected managerial roles in the public and private sectors.
Exceptional Benefits: Public sector and utility company roles often feature some of Canada’s best pension and health plans.
pathways: Express Entry STEM Draws: Direct advantage for managers with engineering backgrounds.
Saskatchewan INP: Targeted draws for utility professionals in the energy sector.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): Addressing infrastructure needs in the Maritimes.
